source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
title: Ensure MFA is enabled for WorkDoc users

input_row.source: CIS AWS END USER COMPUTE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1.2.0
input_row.title: Ensure MFA is enabled for WorkDoc users
input_row.description: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) adds an extra layer of authentication assurance beyond traditional username and password. With MFA enabled, when a user signs in to Amazon WorkDocs, they will be prompted for their user name and password as well as for an authentication code from their MFA token.
input_row.rationale: Enabling MFA provides increased security to a user name and password as it requires the user to possess a solution that displays a time-sensitive authentication code.
input_row.audit: Perform the steps below to confirm MFA setup and configuration. From the console: 1. Log in to the Directory Service console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/directoryservicev2 2. Select Directories. 3. Choose the directory ID link for your AWS Managed Microsoft AD directory. 4. On the Directory details page, select the Networking & security tab. 5. In the Multi-factor authentication section, Confirm Radius status is set to Enabled. 6. Open the WorkDocs console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/zocalo/ 7. In the Manage Your WorkDocs Sites page, select the desired site and choose Actions and Manage MFA. 8. Confirm the values are set correctly. Multi-factor authentication is available when the RADIUS Status reads Enabled. From the Command line: Internal Only - General 1. Run describe-directories command to list the identifiers of all the Active Directory (AD) Connector directories, available in the selected AWS region: aws ds describe-directories --region us-east-1 --output table --query 'DirectoryDescriptions[*].DirectoryId' 2. The command output should return a table with the requested resource IDs: --------------------- |DescribeDirectories| +-------------------+ | d-12345abcde | | d-abcd012345 | | d-aabbcc1234 | +-------------------+ 3. Run describe-directories command using the ID of the AD Connector directory to get the status of the RADIUS MFA server connection: aws ds describe-directories --region us-east-1 --directory-ids d-12345abcde --query 'DirectoryDescriptions[*].RadiusStatus' 4. The command output should return the requested status information: [] 5. Repeat steps 3 and 4 to determine the MFA status for other AD Connector directories. 6. Change the AWS region by updating the --region command parameter value and repeat steps 1 – 5 to perform the audit process for other regions. If describe-directories command output returns an empty array, as shown in the example above, there is no RADIUS MFA server configured for the selected AD Directory, therefore the resource does not have Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) protection enabled. Refer to the remediation below.
input_row.remediation: Perform the following steps to setup MFA on the server and in WorkDocs. From the Console: Internal Only - General 1. Identify the IP address of your RADIUS MFA server and your AWS Managed Microsoft AD directory. 2. In the AWS Directory Service console navigation pane, select Directories. 3. Choose the directory ID link for your AWS Managed Microsoft AD directory. 4. On the Directory details page, select the Networking & security tab. 5. In the Multi-factor authentication section, choose Actions, and then choose Enable. 6. On the Enable multi-factor authentication (MFA) page, provide the following values: - Display label - Provide a label name. - RADIUS server DNS name or IP addresses - Port - default 1812 - Shared secret code - Confirm shared secret code To enable multi-factor authentication in WorkDocs: 1. Open WorkDocs console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/zocalo/ 2. In the Manage Your WorkDocs Sites page, select the desired site and choose Actions and Manage MFA. 3. Enter the following values: - Enable Multi-Factor Authentication - Check to enable multi-factor authentication. - RADIUS server IP address(es) - The IP addresses of your RADIUS server endpoints - Port - The port that your RADIUS server is using for communications. Default RADIUS server port (1812) - Shared secret code - The shared secret code that was specified when your RADIUS endpoints were created. - Confirm shared secret code - Protocol - MS-CHAPv2 - Server timeout - (in seconds) - 20 - Max retries - 3 4. Choose Enable. Internal Only - General Multi-factor authentication is available when the RADIUS Status changes to Enabled. To enable RADIUS-based MFA protection for your Active Directory (AD) Connector directories, perform the following actions: Note: Enabling Multi-Factor authentication for AD Connector directories using the AWS Management Console is not currently supported. From the Command line: 1. Run the enable-radius command: aws ds enable-radius --region us-east-1 --directory-id <value> --radius-settings { "RadiusServers": ["radius.<your-radius-server>.com"],"RadiusPort": 1812,"RadiusTimeout": 20,"RadiusRetries": 3,"SharedSecret": "radiusmfa","AuthenticationProtocol": "MS-CHAPv2","DisplayLabel": "RADIUS Multi-Factor Authentication","UseSameUsername": true } 2. Repeat step 1 for other AD Connectors and the Selected regions.
input_row.impact: To enable MFA for Amazon WorkDocs you require a RADIUS server or a plugin to a RADIUS server already implemented in your environment. Multi-factor authentication is not available for Simple AD. You can enable multi-factor authentication for AD Connector if you have Active Directory running on-premises or in EC2 instances.
input_row.justification: While the first opinion suggests automation via IAM APIs, the second opinion correctly points out that Amazon WorkDocs users are not always managed as IAM users; they may be provisioned via AWS Directory Service, SAML, or other identity providers. AWS APIs (including boto3 and AWS CLI) do not provide a direct method to enumerate all WorkDocs users and check their MFA status across all possible identity sources. Therefore, a reliable, comprehensive automated check is not feasible.
input_row.manual_summary: 1. Log in to the AWS Management Console as an administrator. 2. Navigate to the Amazon WorkDocs console. 3. Select the relevant WorkDocs site. 4. Review the user directory to identify all active WorkDocs users. 5. Determine the identity provider in use (AWS Directory Service, SAML, or IAM). 6. For AWS Directory Service or SAML, access the respective identity provider’s management console (e.g., Microsoft Active Directory, Okta, ADFS). 7. Verify that MFA is enforced for all users who have access to WorkDocs, according to the identity provider’s MFA policy settings. 8. Document the MFA enforcement policy and provide evidence (e.g., screenshots or policy exports) that MFA is enabled for all WorkDocs users. 9. Confirm compliance by ensuring that all user accounts require MFA at login.
input_row.default_value: By default, MFA is not enabled in AWS Workdocs.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:** 
The audit control for enabling Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for WorkDocs users relies on human judgment and action. It involves verifying configurations in the Amazon WorkDocs and Directory Service consoles and potentially checking configurations within the identity provider systems, which are not fully accessible via AWS APIs. Verification also requires logging into different management consoles depending on the identity provider, which adds complexity and further supports the manual approach.

**MANUAL STEPS:** 
1. **Log in to AWS Management Console**: Start by signing in to the AWS Management Console as an administrator.

2. **Navigate to Directory Service**: In the AWS console, go to the Directory Service console by searching for "Directory Service" or directly through the URL https://console.aws.amazon.com/directoryservicev2.

3. **Select Directories**: Once in the Directory Service console, select "Directories" from the navigation pane on the left side.

4. **Open Directory Details**: Click on the directory ID link for your AWS Managed Microsoft AD directory.

5. **Access Networking & Security Tab**: On the Directory details page, navigate to the "Networking & security" tab.

6. **Check RADIUS Status**: In the Multi-factor authentication section, verify that the RADIUS status is set to "Enabled."

7. **Open WorkDocs Console**: Now, navigate to the Amazon WorkDocs console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/zocalo/.

8. **Manage WorkDocs Sites**: In the "Manage Your WorkDocs Sites" page, select the desired WorkDocs site, click on "Actions," and then "Manage MFA."

9. **Confirm MFA Settings**: Review the configuration settings for Multi-factor Authentication. Ensure that MFA is enabled and that the RADIUS server details are correctly configured.

10. **Determine Identity Provider**: Verify the identity provider in use for accessing WorkDocs (AWS Directory Service, SAML, or IAM). 

11. **Verify Identity Provider Configuration (if applicable)**:
    - For AWS Directory Service: Check within the AWS Directory Service console as done in previous steps.
    - For SAML: Access the SAML identity provider management console (e.g., Microsoft Active Directory, Okta, ADFS) and ensure MFA is enforced for WorkDocs users.

12. **Document MFA Enforcement Policy**: Collect evidence of MFA enforcement through screenshots or export policies from the identity provider’s settings.

13. **Compliance Confirmation**: Ensure that documentation reflects that MFA is required for all user accounts that can access WorkDocs.

14. **Repeat for All Regions/Directories**: If you manage multiple AWS regions or directories, ensure to repeat this verification process for each.

By following these steps, you can confirm whether MFA is properly enabled for all WorkDocs users, ensuring compliance with the audit control.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- The manual approach is correct to an extent, as certain aspects of verifying MFA for WorkDocs users, particularly those involving third-party identity providers, may not be fully accessible via AWS APIs. However, AWS APIs and CLI can be used to automate parts of this process, such as checking the RADIUS status in AWS Directory Service.
- AWS Directory Service and WorkDocs configurations can be queried using AWS CLI or boto3, specifically for checking configurations related to AWS Managed Microsoft AD.
- AWS Config can be used to monitor changes in configuration and compliance, potentially automating the detection of non-compliance with MFA requirements.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 6

- The manual steps are generally accurate but could be more streamlined with the use of AWS CLI or boto3 for certain checks.
- The steps lack specific AWS CLI commands or boto3 methods that could be used to automate parts of the process. For example, using `boto3.client('ds')` to describe directories and check RADIUS status.
- The program name is not provided in the assessment. A more descriptive program name following the convention could be `aws_workdocs_mfa_compliance_check`.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Automation Opportunities:** 
  - Use `boto3.client('ds').describe_directories()` to programmatically check the RADIUS status for AWS Managed Microsoft AD.
  - Use AWS Config rules to monitor MFA settings for AWS Directory Service.
  - Consider using AWS CLI commands like `aws ds describe-directories` to fetch directory details.
  
- **Program Name Suggestion:** 
  - `aws_workdocs_mfa_compliance_check` would be descriptive and clear, indicating the specific security check being performed.

- **Missing Considerations:**
  - The assessment does not mention AWS Config, which could be used to automate compliance checks.
  - Consider integrating with AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO) if applicable, as it provides native MFA capabilities.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- While the manual approach is justified for certain checks, there are clear opportunities for automation using AWS APIs and services that were not fully explored in the assessment.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Modify:** 
  - Incorporate AWS CLI and boto3 methods to automate parts of the compliance check, specifically for AWS Directory Service configurations.
  - Suggest using AWS Config to continuously monitor compliance.
  - Provide a descriptive program name for clarity and adherence to naming conventions.
